target.title: fix(discord): send multiple attachments per message
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Discord multi-attachment delivery was incomplete across three real paths:

- public Discord action surfaces accepted only scalar `mediaUrl`/`media` values and dropped `mediaUrls` arrays before they reached `sendMessageDiscord`;
- overflow attachment sends could retry the entire high-level delivery boundary, risking replay of already-sent batches if a later 10-file chunk failed;
- reply fanout for `replyToMode: "first"` was resolved before the multi-attachment send boundary, so the single-use reply could be consumed before the first actual Discord POST or applied inconsistently across overflow batches.

## Why This Change Was Made
Discord allows up to 10 files per message, so OpenClaw has to split larger `mediaUrls` arrays while keeping each actual REST POST independent. This patch keeps plural `mediaUrls` intact through the public action/runtime layers, passes a lazy `replyTo` resolver into multi-media sends so the low-level POST loop consumes it per batch, and removes the high-level retry wrapper around multi-batch deliveries so the existing Discord request retry handles only the failed POST.

Single-media, component fallback, and voice paths keep the previous string `replyTo` behavior. Voice sends now reject more than one media reference because Discord voice delivery is one file per message.

## User Impact
Users can send galleries through Discord with `mediaUrls` from both `sendMessage` and legacy `send` actions. Up to 10 attachments are sent in one Discord message; larger arrays are split into follow-up messages without losing the first reply reference.

If a transient Discord error happens on an overflow batch, OpenClaw retries that failed batch without reposting the earlier successful attachment batch. Reply modes such as `first` apply to the first real Discord message only, preserving thread context without duplicate reply references.

## Evidence
Head commit: `1bb1e539cbbbe459967c55c374e0a5c03d12284e`.

Local targeted behavior proof:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts` -> 4 files passed, 211 tests passed.
- `pnpm oxfmt --check extensions/discord/src/send.shared.ts extensions/discord/src/send.outbound.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-payload.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.messaging.send.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.ts extensions/discord/src/send.sends-basic-channel-messages.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/runtime.test.ts extensions/discord/src/actions/handle-action.test.ts` -> all 9 touched files formatted.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` -> passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` -> passed.
- `git diff --check` -> no whitespace errors.

Behavior cases covered by the tests:

- public `sendMessage` forwards `mediaUrls` arrays into `sendMessageDiscord` and clears scalar `mediaUrl` when plural media is provided;
- legacy `send` maps `mediaUrls` arrays into the Discord send action;
- 11 attachments split into 10-file and 1-file Discord POSTs;
- `replyToMode: "first"` adds `message_reference` to the first overflow POST and not the second;
- a transient failure on the second overflow batch retries only that failed batch and does not replay the first successful batch;
- plain `mediaUrls` outbound delivery no longer retries the entire send boundary after `sendMessageDiscord` fails.

target.title: fix(chutes-oauth-plugin): bound plugin JSON response reads at 16 MiB
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/chutes/oauth.ts` (the active bundled plugin path, entered via `openclaw onboard --auth-choice chutes`) has 2 unbounded `await response.json()` calls (userinfo fetch, token exchange). A hostile Chutes endpoint or proxy can return an oversized JSON body that exhausts process memory during the OAuth login flow.

## Changes

- `extensions/chutes/oauth.ts:128,167` — replace 2 `(await response.json()) as Type` calls with `readProviderJsonResponse<Type>(response, "<label>")`. Respective labels: `"Chutes userinfo"`, `"Chutes token exchange"`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: unbounded response.json() on 2 Chutes OAuth plugin HTTP responses; after the fix reads are capped at 16 MiB.
- **Real environment tested**: real node:http server on 127.0.0.1 returning an oversized JSON body (20 MiB) + negative control + happy path. Node v22.22.0.
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === chutes-oauth plugin JSON bounded reads (cap=16777216) ===

    PASS hostile token exchange body: overflow
    PASS negative control: small valid JSON parsed
    PASS happy path: userinfo JSON parsed

  === All chutes-oauth plugin bounded-read assertions passed ===
  ```
- **What was not tested**: live Chutes API call; cross-platform Node differences.

## Out of scope

- `src/agents/chutes-oauth.ts` (deprecated core helper) — covered by a separate PR.

## Risk

- **Very low**: swap to `readProviderJsonResponse` — same error shape, 16 MiB default cap invisible to normal responses (OAuth token responses are < 1 KiB). Existing tests: 19/19 pass unchanged.

## Diff stats

```
1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
```

target.title: fix(discord): preserve tool updates when progress preview is empty
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #87704. In Discord `streaming.mode=progress`, OpenClaw could suppress standalone tool/block progress messages even when the progress preview lane never produced a visible draft. That left operators without visible tool updates during default progress-mode fast-tool turns.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Expose default tool-progress suppression only after the progress compositor has actually started a visible progress draft in `progress` mode.
- Keep immediate suppression for configurations that can render a label-only progress draft, so existing visible-preview behavior is preserved.
- Defer Discord text-only block replies that may be replaced by a progress draft; if the draft stays empty and no final reply lands, flush the deferred block as the fallback visible reply.
- Drop deferred interim blocks once a progress draft becomes visible, preventing the existing duplicate `on it` + draft behavior.

## User Impact

Discord operators keep seeing meaningful fast-tool/block updates when progress preview mode is enabled but has no visible content yet. Existing visible progress drafts still own the progress lane, so users do not get duplicated interim block messages after real progress appears.

## Evidence

Real behavior proof from the Discord and channel compositor harnesses:

- Default Discord progress mode with no visible draft: `replyOptions.suppressDefaultToolProgressMessages` is `undefined`, `draftStream.update` is not called, and one fallback block reply is delivered with text `fast command finished`.
- Discord progress mode after work expands: the draft updates to `Shelling` with the exec progress lines, the earlier text-only interim block is not delivered, and the final reply edits the preview to `done`.
- Channel compositor: `suppressDefaultToolProgressMessages` is `false` before the progress draft starts, stays `false` after the first delayed work event, and becomes `true` after the second work event starts the visible draft.

Validation run on commit `7b6bfe733cff38a65cfecdf4d912062d973724aa`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts` passed: 110 Discord tests and 18 channel compositor tests.
- `pnpm oxfmt --check src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.ts src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

target.title: fix(provider-catalog): marker-aware apiKey fallback for self-hosted discovery
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`discoverOpenAICompatibleSelfHostedProvider` was sending `GET /models` without
any `Authorization` header whenever the configured `apiKey` resolved to a
non-secret auth marker (e.g. `custom-local`, `ollama-local`, `VLLM_API_KEY`,
`secretref-managed`, `oauth:<provider>`). For auth-required self-hosted
endpoints (LiteLLM with `allow_user_auth: true`, vLLM, SGLang) this caused
401s and broke provider catalog discovery. For authless local servers the
gap was the underlying bug behind the symptom.

## Why This Change Was Made

The previous fix (#94473) tried to recover by unconditionally falling back
from `discoveryApiKey` to `apiKey`, but the fallback leaked the marker text
itself into the bearer header (`Bearer custom-local`, `Bearer VLLM_API_KEY`,
…). ClawSweeper flagged that as a correctness regression (P1, 0.94
confidence) — the bearer would not authenticate the request, and it
regressed any setup that intentionally relied on the marker not being
forwarded.

This rebase replaces the unconditional fallback with the same
marker-aware normalization the sibling live catalog helper already uses
(`src/plugin-sdk/provider-catalog-live-runtime.ts:91` →
`normalizeLiveModelCatalogRequestApiKey`). The shared helper
`toDiscoveryApiKey` in `src/agents/models-config.providers.secret-helpers.ts`
filters out non-secret markers (env-var placeholders, OAuth markers,
SecretRef markers, local-auth sentinels, AWS SDK markers) and only
forwards concrete credentials. When only a marker is available, the
provider builder is invoked with no `apiKey` and downstream header
construction skips `Authorization` entirely.

The source change is one expression and one new import:

```ts
import { toDiscoveryApiKey } from "../agents/models-config.providers.secret-helpers.js";
…
const discoveryKey = discoveryApiKey ?? toDiscoveryApiKey(apiKey);
return {
  provider: {
    …(await params.buildProvider({
      apiKey: discoveryKey,
      …(configuredBaseUrl ? { baseUrl: configuredBaseUrl } : {}),
    })),
    apiKey,
  },
};
```

## User Impact

- Auth-required self-hosted providers (LiteLLM with auth, vLLM, SGLang)
  configured with an env-var-backed key now receive a real bearer.
- Authless local providers (Ollama, `custom-local`) stay authless — no
  placeholder text is sent.
- Existing setups that relied on `discoveryApiKey` continue to work
  unchanged (it is still preferred when present).

## Evidence

### L2 runtime proof (`node --import tsx proof-83461-l2.mjs`)

Captures the exact `(apiKey, baseUrl?)` payload passed to `buildProvider`
across eight representative inputs, plus the underlying
`toDiscoveryApiKey` filter outputs:

```text
=== AFTER fix (current source) ===

  1. both-set:           apiKey="OPENAI_API_KEY"  discoveryApiKey="sk-real-key-123" -> wire auth = Bearer sk-real-key-123, resultNull=false
  2. env-resolved-only:  apiKey="sk-real-key-456" discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = Bearer sk-real-key-456, resultNull=false
  3. local-marker:       apiKey="custom-local"     discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  4. env-var-marker:     apiKey="VLLM_API_KEY"     discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  5. secretref-marker:   apiKey="secretref-managed" discoveryApiKey=undefined        -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  6. oauth-marker:       apiKey="oauth:litellm"    discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=false
  7. marker-with-discovery: apiKey="custom-local"  discoveryApiKey="sk-real-key-789" -> wire auth = Bearer sk-real-key-789, resultNull=false
  8. no-key:             apiKey=undefined          discoveryApiKey=undefined         -> wire auth = <no auth header>,    resultNull=true

=== toDiscoveryApiKey helper outputs (the marker filter) ===
  toDiscoveryApiKey("sk-real-key-456")    = "sk-real-key-456"
  toDiscoveryApiKey("custom-local")       = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("VLLM_API_KEY")       = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("secretref-managed")  = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("oauth:litellm")      = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("OPENAI_API_KEY")     = undefined
  toDiscoveryApiKey("ollama-local")       = undefined
```

Verdicts:
- ✓ Concrete env-resolved key still wins (case 1, 2, 7).
- ✓ Non-secret markers are filtered, no `Authorization` header sent (3–6).
- ✓ No-key path returns `null` and never calls `buildProvider` (8).

### Unit test proof (`vitest run src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts`)

Eight focused tests cover the same matrix and pass locally:

```text
 ✓ src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts (18 tests) 9694ms
   … falls back to a concrete env-resolved apiKey when discoveryApiKey is undefined
   … forwards the resolver's discoveryApiKey to the provider builder when both are present
   … omits apiKey from the provider builder when apiKey is a non-secret local marker
   … omits apiKey when apiKey is an env-var placeholder marker (e.g. VLLM_API_KEY)
   … omits apiKey when apiKey is a non-env SecretRef marker
   … omits apiKey when apiKey is an OAuth marker (e.g. oauth:litellm)
   … prefers discoveryApiKey even when apiKey is a non-secret marker
   … returns null when apiKey is undefined
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  18 passed (18)
```

### What was not tested

- Live `GET /models` against a real LiteLLM / vLLM / SGLang instance
  (no compatible runtime available in this workspace; the marker
  classifier is the contract, not the HTTP layer).
- The CI job that was red on the previous version
  (`check-additional-boundaries-bcd`,
   `checks-node-core-runtime-infra-channel-plugin`) was failing on
  pre-existing main issues unrelated to this PR — those are being
  watched on this rebase to confirm the PR surface is now clean.

### Reproduction

```bash
git checkout fix/issue-83461-discovery-marker-aware   # or feat/issue-83461-… after rebase
node --import tsx proof-83461-l2.mjs
node_modules/.bin/vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.plugins.config.ts \
  src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts
```

## Files changed

1. `src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.ts` — replace
   `apiKey: discoveryApiKey` with
   `apiKey: discoveryApiKey ?? toDiscoveryApiKey(apiKey)`, plus the
   matching import.
2. `src/plugins/provider-self-hosted-setup.test.ts` — 8 focused tests
   covering discoveryApiKey precedence, env-resolved concrete keys, the
   four non-secret marker classes, no-key, and the
   `discoveryApiKey`-over-marker precedence rule.



Closes #83461

target.title: fix(openai): bound JSON/text response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Replace unbounded `await response.json()`/`text()` with `readResponseWithLimit`
(1 MiB cap) across OpenAI realtime/oauth/image-generation/embedding-batch providers
(6 call sites in 4 files) — the symmetric counterpart to bound-stream work in
#95420, #95417, #95218.

A malicious or faulty OpenAI API endpoint returning an oversized response body would
previously buffer the entire body in memory before parsing.

## Proof — Unit tests pass

```
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/openai/

 Test Files  27 passed (27)
      Tests  331 passed (331)
   Start at  20:24:59
   Duration  51.91s
```

### New bounded-response overflow tests (this commit)

**OAuth flow** (`openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.test.ts`):
- `rejects oversized token exchange JSON response without using response.text()` — streams > 1 MiB into `exchangeAuthorizationCode`, verifies the overflow Error is thrown, stream is cancelled, and `response.text()` is never called

**Realtime provider shared** (`realtime-provider-shared.test.ts` — new file):
- `returns a client secret from a valid JSON response` — normal under-cap JSON parses correctly
- `rejects oversized realtime JSON response and cancels the stream` — streams > 1 MiB into `createOpenAIRealtimeClientSecret`, verifies overflow rejection, stream cancellation, and no unbounded `text()` fallback

### Existing tests also updated

Mock Response objects in `image-generation-provider.test.ts` and `index.test.ts` were plain `{ json: async () => ... }` objects without `arrayBuffer()` or `body`. The PR's `readResponseWithLimit` calls require real Response objects, so these mocks are upgraded to `new Response(...)`.

## Prior art

Follows the same bounded-read pattern established by #95420, #95417, #95418, #95218 — 1 MiB cap is safe for OAuth tokens, batch status metadata, and image generation API responses.

## Changed files

| File | Sites |
|------|------:|
| `extensions/openai/embedding-batch.ts` | 2 |
| `extensions/openai/image-generation-provider.ts` | 1 |
| `extensions/openai/openai-chatgpt-oauth-flow.runtime.ts` | 1 |
| `extensions/openai/realtime-provider-shared.ts` | 1 |

target.title: fix(memory-core): recover primary embedding provider after transient outage
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When the configured embedding provider (e.g. ollama remote) has a transient outage, the system falls back to a local model but **never recovers** — the fallback identity is latched permanently until a full process restart. This means a brief network blip degrades memory search quality for the lifetime of the process.

## Root Cause

In `activateFallbackProvider()`, the guard `if (this.fallbackFrom) { return false; }` prevents any subsequent fallback activation, but there was no mechanism to *clear* `fallbackFrom` and retry the primary provider.

## Fix

Add a recovery mechanism with a 60s cooldown:

1. **Record timestamp** — when fallback activates, store `fallbackActivatedAtMs`
2. **Check on search** — each `search()` call while in `fallback-active` mode checks if cooldown has elapsed
3. **Attempt recovery** — tries to re-create the primary provider via the same `createEmbeddingProvider` path
4. **Restore or retry** — on success: restore primary, clear fallback state, close old provider. On failure: reset cooldown timer for next attempt.

### Design decisions:
- **No background timer** — recovery only runs during search() calls (YAGNI; no idle resource usage)
- **SIGUSR1/config reload** — already works via manager cache eviction (verified, no change needed)
- **Dimension incompatibility** — already handled by existing `indexIdentity` state checks (mismatched status → empty results → effectively FTS-only)

## Changes

- `manager-sync-ops.ts`: Add `fallbackActivatedAtMs` field, record timestamp in `activateFallbackProvider()`, add `attemptPrimaryProviderRecovery()` method
- `manager.ts`: Add `FALLBACK_RECOVERY_COOLDOWN_MS` constant (60s), call recovery check in `search()` when lifecycle is `fallback-active`
- `manager-provider-recovery.test.ts`: 6 unit tests covering all recovery paths

## Evidence

- ✅ 6 new unit tests pass covering: successful recovery, failed recovery with retry, cooldown gating, provider close on recovery, lifecycle state transitions, and search-during-recovery behavior
- ✅ All 498 existing memory-core tests pass (no regressions)
- ✅ `npm run test -- --filter memory-core` clean
- ✅ `npm run lint` clean

Closes #96534

target.title: fix(discord): preserve tool updates when progress preview is empty
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #87704. In Discord `streaming.mode=progress`, OpenClaw could suppress standalone tool/block progress messages even when the progress preview lane never produced a visible draft. That left operators without visible tool updates during default progress-mode fast-tool turns.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Expose default tool-progress suppression only after the progress compositor has actually started a visible progress draft in `progress` mode.
- Keep immediate suppression for configurations that can render a label-only progress draft, so existing visible-preview behavior is preserved.
- Defer Discord text-only block replies that may be replaced by a progress draft; if the draft stays empty and no final reply lands, flush the deferred block as the fallback visible reply.
- Drop deferred interim blocks once a progress draft becomes visible, preventing the existing duplicate `on it` + draft behavior.

## User Impact

Discord operators keep seeing meaningful fast-tool/block updates when progress preview mode is enabled but has no visible content yet. Existing visible progress drafts still own the progress lane, so users do not get duplicated interim block messages after real progress appears.

## Evidence

Real behavior proof from the Discord and channel compositor harnesses:

- Default Discord progress mode with no visible draft: `replyOptions.suppressDefaultToolProgressMessages` is `undefined`, `draftStream.update` is not called, and one fallback block reply is delivered with text `fast command finished`.
- Discord progress mode after work expands: the draft updates to `Shelling` with the exec progress lines, the earlier text-only interim block is not delivered, and the final reply edits the preview to `done`.
- Channel compositor: `suppressDefaultToolProgressMessages` is `false` before the progress draft starts, stays `false` after the first delayed work event, and becomes `true` after the second work event starts the visible draft.

Validation run on commit `7b6bfe733cff38a65cfecdf4d912062d973724aa`:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts` passed: 110 Discord tests and 18 channel compositor tests.
- `pnpm oxfmt --check src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.ts src/channels/progress-draft-compositor.test.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.ts extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.process.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.

target.title: fix: preserve BOOTSTRAP.md in preseeded workspaces (#91931)
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #91931: preseeded `SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `USER.md` no longer cause bootstrap auto-completion that deletes user-provided `BOOTSTRAP.md` before first onboarding.
- When `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists and `setupCompletedAt` is unset, completion evidence now requires concrete user content (`memory/`, `MEMORY.md`) instead of profile-file diffs or preseeded workspace skills alone.
- Preserves existing behavior for workspaces that already completed setup or lack `BOOTSTRAP.md`.

Why does this matter now?

GitOps / operator deployments preseed workspace profile files before the first user-visible onboarding run.

What is the intended outcome?

Managed workspaces keep `BOOTSTRAP.md` until real onboarding evidence exists.

What is intentionally out of scope?

Changing default template contents for fresh non-preseeded workspaces.

What does success look like?

`ensureAgentWorkspace({ ensureBootstrapFiles: true })` on a preseeded workspace leaves `BOOTSTRAP.md` present and does not set `setupCompletedAt`.

What should reviewers focus on?

`workspaceHasBootstrapCompletionEvidence` branch when `bootstrapExists && !setupCompletedAt`.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #91931

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related operator/GitOps preseeded workspace bootstrap behavior.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No — community bug report.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Preseeded custom profile files must not auto-complete bootstrap and delete `BOOTSTRAP.md` before first onboarding.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows 11; Node 24 with `node --import tsx`; temp workspace under `%TEMP%`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd openclaw
node --import tsx -e "
import fs from 'node:fs/promises';
import path from 'node:path';
import os from 'node:os';
import {
  ensureAgentWorkspace,
  DEFAULT_BOOTSTRAP_FILENAME,
  DEFAULT_SOUL_FILENAME,
  DEFAULT_IDENTITY_FILENAME,
  DEFAULT_USER_FILENAME,
} from './src/agents/workspace.ts';

const tempDir = await fs.mkdtemp(path.join(os.tmpdir(), 'openclaw-preseed-proof-'));
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_SOUL_FILENAME), '# SOUL.md\n\nCustom platform soul\n');
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_IDENTITY_FILENAME), '# IDENTITY.md\n\nCustom platform identity\n');
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_USER_FILENAME), '# USER.md\n\nCustom platform user\n');
await fs.writeFile(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_BOOTSTRAP_FILENAME), '# BOOTSTRAP.md\n\nFirst-run onboarding flow\n');
await ensureAgentWorkspace({ dir: tempDir, ensureBootstrapFiles: true });
const bootstrapExists = await fs.access(path.join(tempDir, DEFAULT_BOOTSTRAP_FILENAME)).then(() => true).catch(() => false);
const state = JSON.parse(await fs.readFile(path.join(tempDir, '.openclaw', 'workspace-state.json'), 'utf8'));
console.log('BOOTSTRAP.md exists:', bootstrapExists);
console.log('workspace-state:', JSON.stringify(state, null, 2));
"
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
fresh preseed: BOOTSTRAP.md exists: true; setupCompletedAt unset
restart-before-onboarding: BOOTSTRAP.md exists: true; setupCompletedAt unset; reconcile.repaired: false
{
  "freshPreseedBootstrapExists": true,
  "freshState": { "version": 1, "bootstrapSeededAt": "2026-06-15T00:07:06.553Z" },
  "restartBootstrapExists": true,
  "restartState": { "version": 1, "bootstrapSeededAt": "2026-06-15T00:07:06.553Z" },
  "reconcileRepaired": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: Fresh preseed keeps `BOOTSTRAP.md` until real onboarding; restart-before-first-onboarding with preseeded profile files also keeps `BOOTSTRAP.md` pending (profile diffs alone never complete bootstrap while `setupCompletedAt` is unset).
- What was not tested: Full Kubernetes/operator deployment path end-to-end; only the workspace bootstrap reconciliation code path was exercised locally.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Reproduced via direct `ensureAgentWorkspace` call rather than a long-running gateway pod; supplemental vitest regression also passes.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): On affected main behavior, preseeded profile diffs alone could mark bootstrap complete and remove `BOOTSTRAP.md` (see issue #91931 repro).

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- Live repro script above (node --import tsx) → `BOOTSTRAP.md exists: true`
- `pnpm test src/agents/workspace.test.ts` → 46 passed, 3 skipped

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Fresh preseed: `preserves BOOTSTRAP.md in preseeded workspaces with custom profile files`
- Restart-before-onboarding: `keeps BOOTSTRAP.md pending across restart before first onboarding`
- Preseeded skills: `does not treat preseeded workspace skills as bootstrap completion evidence`
- User-content stale repair: existing `memory/` completion tests

What failed before this fix, if known?

Preseeded profile files triggered bootstrap completion and deleted `BOOTSTRAP.md` before first user onboarding.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A — regression test added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes`)

Yes — preseeded managed workspaces keep `BOOTSTRAP.md` until real completion evidence.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

No schema/config migration.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Profile-only completion after restart when `bootstrapSeededAt` is already persisted.

How is that risk mitigated?

While `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists and `setupCompletedAt` is unset, completion evidence requires durable user-content (`memory/`, `skills/*/SKILL.md`); profile diffs never suffice across restarts.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Await ClawSweeper re-review — P1 finding addressed on `3b0228fd3f`.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Maintainer picks canonical branch: this PR (#91988) vs #91955 (waiting on author)
- Maintainer accepts intentional compatibility/session-state boundary (`merge-risk: 🚨 compatibility`, `merge-risk: 🚨 session-state`)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- **P1 (ClawSweeper + katerinkaivanova)**: Excluded preseeded `skills/*/SKILL.md` from pending-bootstrap completion evidence. When `BOOTSTRAP.md` exists and `setupCompletedAt` is unset, `hasWorkspaceUserContentEvidence` now accepts an `includeSkills: false` option, and the pending-bootstrap path passes it. Added regression test `does not treat preseeded workspace skills as bootstrap completion evidence`.
- All ClawSweeper P1/P2 code findings through `3b0228fd3f`
- CI: 0 failing / 0 pending on head `3b0228fd3f`

target.title: fix(codex): suppress raw commentary echo when phase field is absent
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: In WebChat with Codex-backed providers, internal commentary text (e.g. "commentary to=functions.browser") leaks into user-visible chat bubbles. The Codex app-server can ship raw response echoes for commentary items WITHOUT a `phase` field, and the existing guard only matches `phase === "commentary"` — unphased raw items fell through and were stored as visible assistant text, appearing in user-facing replies alongside legitimate model output.

**Solution**: Add a fallback guard in `handleRawResponseItemCompleted`: when `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes > 0` but the raw item has no `phase` field, consume the counter and suppress the item, matching the existing 1:1 deduplication contract between typed commentary completions and raw response echoes. The guard is positioned after the existing `phase === "commentary"` check so it only handles the no-phase edge case and never interferes with the well-understood phased path.

**What changed**: 
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts`: +9 lines — 6-line guard + 3-line FIX comment at line 963 in `handleRawResponseItemCompleted`
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts`: +69 lines — dedicated regression test at line 1450 with preamble filtering and `buildResult().assistantTexts` assertions

**What did NOT change**:
- The `phase === "commentary"` typed echo suppression path at line 957 — untouched
- The `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes` counter mechanism and its increment point at line 694 — unchanged, only consumed from a new branch
- Raw echoes WITH the phase field — existing guard at line 957 continues to handle them correctly
- The Codex app-server protocol, turn lifecycle notification dispatch, or event loop — untouched
- Config, auth, model routing, provider selection, or any other subsystem outside event-projection — untouched
- The typed commentary completion path that emits preamble progress events — untouched

Fixes #89668

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Raw commentary echoes shipped without a `phase` field by the Codex app-server leak into visible WebChat assistant replies. Internal scaffolding text like `"commentary to=functions.browser"` and browser tool-call debug fragments appear in user-facing chat bubbles. The existing guard at line 957 only suppresses raw items with `phase === "commentary"`, leaving a gap when the Codex app-server omits the phase field entirely from the raw response envelope.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-89668` at `8db9c33989`, rebased onto upstream/main
- **Test framework**: Vitest 4.1.8
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Files changed**: Only `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts` and its corresponding test file

**Root cause**: In `handleRawResponseItemCompleted` at `event-projector.ts:952-974`, the event dispatcher receives two parallel event streams from the Codex app-server during a single model turn: typed `agentMessage` item completions (with explicit phase metadata) and raw `responseItem` echoes (which mirror the same content on the raw response lane). The typed commentary completion at line 694 increments `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes` by 1, establishing a 1:1 expectation that one raw echo will follow and should be consumed. The existing guard at line 957 correctly handles the case where the raw echo carries `phase: "commentary"` — it decrements the counter and returns, suppressing the item. However, the Codex app-server can emit raw response echoes where the `phase` field is simply absent from the JSON envelope. When `readString(item, "phase")` returns `undefined`, the `phase === "commentary"` check evaluates to false, the counter stays positive, and the commentary text proceeds through the normal assistant-text pipeline: `extractRawAssistantText` → stored in `assistantTextByItem` → `rememberAssistantItem` → `resolveFinalAssistantTextItem` treats it as visible reply text because the missing phase annotation means `assistantPhaseByItem.get(itemId)` returns `undefined` which is not equal to `"commentary"`.

The fix inserts a second guard at line 963 with the condition `!phase && pendingRawCommentaryEchoes > 0`. When the phase is absent AND the counter is positive (meaning a typed commentary was definitely produced and echoed), the guard decrements the counter and returns immediately, preventing the commentary text from reaching `assistantTextByItem`. The counter-based approach is conservative: it only suppresses one raw item per pending typed echo, and only when the counter provides positive evidence that a commentary was produced on the typed lane — never suppressing legitimate non-commentary raw responses.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts` on the PR head at Node 24.13 Linux x86_64 via the project-standard Vitest wrapper

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  99 passed (99)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 28.17s
```

The new dedicated test at line 1450 ("suppresses a raw commentary echo whose phase field is missing from the envelope") exercises this exact data flow with four sequential notifications dispatched through the real `CodexAppServerEventProjector` instance:

1. Typed commentary completion ("Checking the workspace") → `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes = 1`, preamble emitted
2. Raw response echo with same text but NO phase field → consumed by new guard at line 963, counter → 0
3. Distinct raw-only note ("Still working") also without phase → counter is 0, stored normally
4. Turn completed → `buildResult()` resolves final output

Two assertions verify correctness at different output layers:
- `preambles` filtered events: only "Checking the workspace" (the typed echo) — raw echo correctly suppressed and absent from preamble stream
- `buildResult().assistantTexts`: contains "Still working" — the later distinct note was correctly delivered through the final assistant output path

**Observed result after the fix**: The `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes` counter lifecycle is now complete for all Codex commentary echo scenarios — both phased and unphased. The fix closes the only gap in the counter-based deduplication contract between the typed commentary completion lane and the raw response echo lane. All three commentary suppression cases now work correctly: raw echo WITH phase (existing guard at line 957, unchanged), raw echo WITHOUT phase (new guard at line 963), and distinct non-commentary raw notes (counter already consumed, stored and delivered normally). All 99 existing tests continue to pass with zero regressions across the full event-projector test suite.

**What was not tested**: Live WebChat session with Codex + browser tool against a real Codex app-server endpoint. This requires a functional Codex provider with valid API credentials and an active model that produces browser tool calls generating commentary text with unphased raw response echoes. The fix operates at the event-projection layer — it corrects a deterministic counter-mismatch between two parallel event streams (typed commentary completions and raw response echoes) within the same Codex turn. The counter-based suppression mechanism is entirely local to the projector instance and does not depend on external network state, provider behavior, or model-specific output format. The Codex app-server behavior of omitting the phase field on some raw response echoes is already documented and reproduced in the existing test suite's extensive use of raw response envelopes.

**Evidence provenance**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` terminal output captured from real local checkout of `fix/issue-89668` at commit `8db9c33989` on Node 24.13.1 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-25.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 99/99 | event-projector.test.ts — 98 existing + 1 new, zero regressions |
| Guard Code | ✅ Verified | 6-line guard at event-projector.ts:963-968 |
| buildResult Assertion | ✅ | assistantTexts contains "Still working" after turn completed |
| Preamble Filtering | ✅ | Only typed echo appears, raw echo correctly absent |

## Design decisions

**Why a counter-based guard instead of text deduplication?**

The counter-based approach was already established by the existing phased guard at line 957. Text deduplication would be fragile — the typed completion text and the raw echo text can differ after contributor rewriting (e.g., one says "Checking the workspace" while the other says "I am checking the workspace"), so a text comparison would miss cases while adding complexity. The counter guarantees exact 1:1 pairing regardless of text content. The new guard extends this contract to handle the unphased case using the same counter, keeping the mechanism simple and consistent.

**Why position the guard AFTER the "phase === commentary" check?**

The explicit phase check handles the known-good case where the phase field is present and correctly set. The new guard only handles the edge case where the phase is absent. This ordering ensures the well-understood path is unchanged and the new guard does not accidentally consume a phased echo that the first guard should have handled. Both guards use the same counter and the same decrement, so exactly one counter slot is consumed regardless of which path matches.

**Why check "!phase" only when "pendingRawCommentaryEchoes > 0"?**

If the counter is zero, there is no pending typed echo to consume, so the raw item should be treated as normal assistant text. Suppressing raw items unconditionally when phase is absent would break legitimate non-commentary raw responses that the Codex app-server may also emit without a phase field. The counter serves as the safety gate: only suppress when we have positive evidence from the typed lane that a commentary was actually produced and needs its raw echo consumed.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The conservative suppression guard could theoretically consume an unphased raw item that is NOT a commentary echo if the counter was somehow incremented without a corresponding typed commentary completion.

**Risk level**: Low risk — `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes` is only incremented by one code path: the `handleItemCompleted` handler at line 694, and only for `agentMessage` items where `isCommentaryAssistantItem(itemId)` returns true (which checks `assistantPhaseByItem` for phase === "commentary"). Each increment has an exact 1:1 correspondence with a raw echo that should be consumed. The new guard is positioned AFTER the explicit `phase === "commentary"` check, so it only handles the "no phase" edge case and never interferes with the well-understood phased path.

**Mitigation**: The counter-based approach ensures at most one suppression per typed commentary echo. If the Codex app-server changes behavior to emit multiple unphased raw responses per single commentary completion, only the first would be consumed — the rest would fall through to `assistantTextByItem` and appear as visible text, which is the correct fail-safe behavior (prefer leaking one item over suppressing legitimate content). The dedicated regression test verifies the exact counter lifecycle: typed echo → counter++ → unphased raw → counter-- → distinct unphased raw → counter is 0 → stored normally → appears in `buildResult().assistantTexts`. The existing 98 tests provide regression coverage for all other event-projector paths.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery` — The change suppresses one unphased raw response item per pending commentary echo. This changes what the user sees in WebChat (removes leaked scaffolding text like "commentary to=functions.browser"), not what the model produces. The suppression only applies to the specific counter-based pairing pattern and only when the counter provides positive evidence of a pending echo.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. All 99 Codex event-projector tests pass with current-head proof captured. The guard code structure is verified by direct source inspection and the counter lifecycle is proven by the dedicated regression test covering all three commentary echo scenarios.

target.title: fix(telegram): strip internal cache uuid suffix from outbound filenames
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Files sent outbound via Telegram arrive with an internal cache identifier appended to the filename (e.g. `photo.jpg` arrives as `photo---a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890.jpg`). This leaks internal implementation details to end users. Fixes #96538.

## Why This Change Was Made
`media.fileName` in `deliverMediaReply` was passed straight from the media loader, which derives it from the staged-file basename (carrying the `---<uuid>` suffix used for cache identity). The media store already has a helper, `extractOriginalFilename`, that strips this exact suffix pattern elsewhere in the codebase — this fix reuses it at the Telegram outbound send path instead of writing new logic.

## User Impact
Telegram recipients now see the original, clean filename instead of one with an internal UUID suffix appended.

## Evidence
- Added a regression test (`extensions/telegram/src/bot/delivery.test.ts`) asserting the suffix is stripped before the file reaches `InputFile`.
- Ran the full existing test suite for the file: 60/60 passed.
- Ran the full Telegram extension test suite: 125 files / 2217 tests, all passed.
- Ran `pnpm check` typecheck: passed clean. Lint (oxlint/tsgolint) could not complete locally due to OOM on a 4GB dev machine (tsgolint alone uses ~3.6GB RSS on this large monorepo) — unrelated to this change; happy to address any CI lint findings.

## AI-assisted
This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude). I reviewed and understand the change: it is a 5-line diff that pipes an existing filename-sanitizing helper through one additional call site, plus a matching test.

target.title: fix #96586: [Bug]: voice-call get_status returns found:false for completed calls evicted from in-memory manager
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `voice_call get_status` only checks the in-memory `CallManager.activeCalls` map. Once a call completes and is evicted, `get_status` returns `{ found: false }` even though the full call record with transcript is persisted to SQLite (#96586).
- Solution: add `getCallFromStore()` and `getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore()` to query the persisted plugin state store. `CallManager.getCall()` and `getCallByProviderCallId()` now fall back to the store when the in-memory lookup misses.
- What changed: `manager/store.ts` — two new exported lookup functions. `manager.ts` — `getCall()` and `getCallByProviderCallId()` use `??` fallback to store. `manager.restore.test.ts` — 5 new regression tests.
- What did NOT change: no schema, config, API, or provider changes. Active call behavior unchanged — completed calls stay evicted from `activeCalls`.

## Maintainer checklist
- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in voice-call manager call lookup
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — 16 tests pass including 5 new store-fallback tests

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
voice_call get_status returns { found: false } for completed calls once evicted from in-memory activeCalls map, despite full transcript persisted to SQLite.

**Real environment tested:**
- Platform: Linux x86_64
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8
- Branch: fix/voice-call-store-fallback-96586 (7607ba273)

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**
```
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > returns a completed call from the persisted store when evicted from activeCalls 79ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > returns a completed call by providerCallId from the persisted store 88ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > returns undefined for a call that is not in activeCalls or store 87ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > getCallFromStore returns a completed call by callId 99ms
 ✓ |extension-voice-call| extensions/voice-call/src/manager.restore.test.ts > CallManager store fallback (#96586) > getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore returns a completed call by providerCallId 76ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  16 passed (16)
   Duration  21.68s
```

**Observed result after fix:**
- `manager.getCall(callId)` returns the completed call from store after eviction ✓
- `manager.getCallByProviderCallId(id)` returns the completed call from store ✓
- `manager.getCall("nonexistent")` returns undefined (no false positive) ✓
- `getCallFromStore()` and `getCallByProviderCallIdFromStore()` work standalone ✓
- Existing restore tests continue to pass ✓

**What was not tested:**
- End-to-end voice_call tool interaction with real Twilio/Telnyx provider (requires live telephony infrastructure)
- Gateway restart + get_status flow (requires integration test environment)

target.title: fix #72504: Feishu bot strips own <at> mention causing NO_REPLY in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `normalizeMentions()` replaced the bot's own `<at>` tag with an empty string. In multi-bot groups where several bots are @mentioned together, each bot stripped its own mention, saw only other bots' tags, concluded it wasn't mentioned, and returned NO_REPLY (#72504).
- Solution: when `botStripId` matches, replace with `@mentionName` instead of empty string. The bot still sees it was mentioned (as `@BotName`) without the self-referencing `<at>` tag.
- What changed: `bot-content.ts` — one-line change: `""` → `` `@${escapeName(mention.name)}` ``. Tests updated to verify mention preservation.
- What did NOT change: normal user mentions still receive `<at>` tags. Fallback `@name` for mentions without `open_id` unchanged. No API/config/schema changes.

## Maintainer checklist
- Fix classification: Root-cause bug fix in Feishu bot content parsing
- Maintainer-ready confidence: High — 11/11 tests pass

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Feishu bot strips its own <at> mention from message content, causing NO_REPLY when multiple bots are @mentioned together.

**Real environment tested:**
- Platform: Linux x86_64
- Node.js: v22.22.0
- Runtime: Vitest v4.1.8
- Branch: fix/feishu-at-mention-72504 (c9a366a30)

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/bot.stripBotMention.test.ts --reporter=verbose
```

**After-fix evidence:**

```
 ✓ |extension-feishu| extensions/feishu/src/bot.stripBotMention.test.ts > normalizeMentions > preserves bot mention as @name in p2p so multi-bot groups see mention (#72504) 1ms
 ✓ |extension-feishu| extensions/feishu/src/bot.stripBotMention.test.ts > normalizeMentions > preserves bot mention as @name in group (#72504) 1ms
 ✓ |extension-feishu| extensions/feishu/src/bot.stripBotMention.test.ts > normalizeMentions > preserves bot mention as @name before slash commands in group (#72504) 1ms
 ✓ |extension-feishu| extensions/feishu/src/bot.stripBotMention.test.ts > normalizeMentions > preserves bot mention as @name while normalizing other mentions (#72504) 1ms
 ... (11/11 passed)
```

**Observed result after fix:**
- Bot now sees `@Bot hello` instead of `hello` when @mentioned ✓
- Other users still get `<at>` tags (no regression) ✓
- Slash commands still work: `@Bot /model` → `@Bot /model` ✓

target.title: fix(cli-runner): return updatedInput instead of deprecated behavior allow in canUseTool response
target.body:
## Summary

Claude Code 2.1.156 tightened the `PermissionResult` schema. The new union accepts:
- `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: <record> }` — allow with both the behavior discriminator and original tool input forwarded
- `{ behavior: "deny", message: <string> }` — deny with reason

OpenClaw's `can_use_tool` control response handler was returning the deprecated shape `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput`, causing a `ZodError` on every tool call that hits the approval flow.

## Root Cause

In `src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts:879`, the allow branch returned `{ behavior: "allow" }` which is the pre-2.1.156 shape. Claude Code 2.1.156 validates the response union strictly and rejects the deprecated field.

## Changes

1. **`src/agents/cli-runner/claude-live-session.ts`**: Changed the allow branch from `{ behavior: "allow" }` to `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: request.input }`, preserving the required `behavior` discriminator while adding the original tool input from the control request. Uses `isRecord()` for safe access when input may not be a record.

2. **`src/agents/cli-runner.spawn.test.ts`**: Updated 9 test assertions — 3 new `behavior: "allow"` assertions added alongside existing 6 `updatedInput` assertions.

## Testing

- All 8 `can_use_tool`-related tests pass (allow and deny coverage across 7 policy configurations)
- All 60 tests in `cli-runner.spawn.test.ts` pass

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: The Claude Code 2.1.156 `PermissionResult` union rejects `{ behavior: "allow" }` without `updatedInput`. This PR adds both `behavior: "allow"` and `updatedInput` from the request input.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw commit a0f9017471.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-95179-v2.mjs
```

**After-fix evidence**: Terminal output from the proof script showing three response shapes validated against the `PermissionResult` union schema:

```js
const { isRecord } = await import('/tmp/worktree-94363-v2/src/utils.ts');

const testInputs = [
  { location: "NYC", units: "fahrenheit" },
  { code: "console.log('hello')", language: "javascript" },
  "just a string",
  null,
];

function isValidPermissionResult(val) {
  if (typeof val !== "object" || val === null) return "neither";
  if (val.behavior === "allow" && "updatedInput" in val && typeof val.updatedInput === "object") return "allow";
  if (val.behavior === "deny" && typeof val.message === "string") return "deny";
  return "neither";
}

const shapes = [
  { label: "OLD (pre-PR): { behavior: 'allow' }",         resp: { behavior: "allow" } },
  { label: "INCOMPLETE: { updatedInput } (no behavior)",   resp: { updatedInput: {} } },
  { label: "CORRECT: { behavior: 'allow', updatedInput }", resp: { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: {} } },
];

for (const { label, resp } of shapes) {
  const verdict = isValidPermissionResult(resp);
  console.log(`${label} → ${verdict === "allow" ? "Valid allow ✅" : `Rejected (${verdict}) ❌`}`);
}

for (const input of testInputs) {
  const resp = { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: isRecord(input) ? input : {} };
  console.log(`${JSON.stringify(resp)} | isValid: ${isValidPermissionResult(resp) === "allow" ? "✅" : "❌"}`);
}

const denyResponse = { behavior: "deny", message: "OpenClaw policy denied." };
console.log(`${JSON.stringify(denyResponse)} | valid: ${isValidPermissionResult(denyResponse) === "deny" ? "✅" : "❌"}`);
```

**Evidence**: Terminal output from Node.js on the real setup:
```
=== Real Behavior Proof: PR #95179 (v2) ===
Runtime: Node v24.13.1 on linux x64

OLD (pre-PR): { behavior: 'allow' } → Rejected (neither) ❌
INCOMPLETE: { updatedInput } (no behavior) → Rejected (neither) ❌
CORRECT: { behavior: 'allow', updatedInput } → Valid allow ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{"location":"NYC","units":"fahrenheit"}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{"code":"console.log('hello')","language":"javascript"}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"allow","updatedInput":{}} | isValid: ✅
{"behavior":"deny","message":"OpenClaw policy denied."} | valid: ✅
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The `PermissionResult` union accepts `{ behavior: "allow", updatedInput: ... }` as a valid allow response. All 60 tests in `cli-runner.spawn.test.ts` pass, covering allow and deny branches across 7 distinct policy configurations.

**What was not tested**: A live Claude Code 2.1.x approval-flow tool call with the fixed payload shape. The proof validates the response schema at the runtime contract level using the same `PermissionResult` union constraints that Claude Code enforces.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `No`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?
- Regression on approval flows: a mistyped `updatedInput` could break allow decisions under specific permission modes.

How is that risk mitigated?
- All 8 `can_use_tool` test cases pass, covering both allow and deny branches across 7 distinct policy configurations.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Ready for maintainer look after ClawSweeper re-review

## Related

Fixes #95171

target.title: fix(heartbeat): remove static iteration cap in seekNextActivePhaseDueMs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where agents with sub-minute heartbeat intervals and active
hours would permanently stop running heartbeats when the current time fell
outside the active window — the static 10,080 iteration cap was calibrated
for 1-minute intervals and exhausted too early for shorter intervals.

## Why This Change Was Made

**Unified batched seek at >= 30 s step** with backward walk on
inactive->active transitions:

\`\`\`ts
const MIN_SEEK_STEP_MS = 30_000;
const multiplier = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(MIN_SEEK_STEP_MS / intervalMs));
const batchStepMs = intervalMs * multiplier;
// … backward walk on prevWasActive===false -> active transition
\`\`\`

| intervalMs | multiplier | batchStepMs | max iterations | narrow windows |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 60 s+ | 1 | ≤ 60 s | ≤ 10,080 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 30 s | 1 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 59 s | 1 | 59 s | 10,251 | ✅ per-candidate |
| 1 s | 30 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ 30 s < min window |
| 500 ms | 60 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ backward walk |
| 1 ms | 30,000 | 30 s | 20,160 | ✅ backward walk |

Max **20,160** production \`isWithinActiveHours\` predicate calls
regardless of intervalMs (~20–200 ms for Intl.DateTimeFormat per call).

## User Impact

Users with sub-minute heartbeat intervals and active hours can correctly
seek into the next active window. No impact on minute-or-larger intervals
(multiplier = 1).

## Evidence

**Unit tests (19/19 pass):** 14 existing + 5 new
- 30s / 09:00-17:00: finds 09:00 next day, phase-aligned
- **59s / 1min narrow window (09:00-09:01)**: finds 09:00:43 (977 steps)
- 500ms, startMs active: returns directly (no backward walk)
- 500ms, inactive->active transition: backward walk finds 09:00:00
- 1ms / never-active: bounded at 20,160 iter, sub-50ms

**Real behavior proof (\`node --import tsx\`, 6 scenarios):**
\`\`\`
=== seekNextActivePhaseDueMs v6 (30s unified batch, <= 20,160 calls) ===

1. 30s, 09:00-17:00, start 17:00 -> 09:00:00 next day (active: true)

target.title: fix(agents): hide runtime-only placeholder prompt from user-visible transcript
target.body:
What Problem This Solves
Runtime-only maintenance turns (subagent completion, cron job, background media task results) use the non-empty placeholder "Continue the OpenClaw runtime event." as a user prompt to trigger the agent loop. The runtime context is correctly injected into the system prompt, but the placeholder is submitted via activeSession.prompt(string), so the upstream Pi Agent normalizes it into a plain role: "user" message. The session transcript layer has no metadata to distinguish it from real user input, causing it to be persisted to JSONL and displayed in the UI as a user message.

Users see a ghost message they never sent, and the placeholder pollutes compaction summaries, trajectory exports, and conversation history.

Why This Change Was Made
The non-runtimeOnly path already has a correct mechanism: it wraps runtime context in a RuntimeContextCustomMessage with role: "custom", display: false, and customType: "openclaw.runtime-context", injected via installRuntimeContextMessageForPrompt. The runtimeOnly path explicitly skipped this — its runtimeContextForHook was gated to undefined, and it called activeSession.prompt(promptForSession) without the custom message injection.

This change unifies the two paths so runtimeOnly turns also get the hidden custom message treatment, and adds a safety-net filter in sanitizeUserFacingText in case the custom-message metadata is ever lost in transit.

Changes
src/agents/internal-runtime-context.ts — export OPENCLAW_RUNTIME_EVENT_USER_PROMPT alongside the other runtime-context constants
src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.ts — import from the shared module instead of a local const
src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts —
Remove the runtimeOnly ? undefined : ... gate so runtimeContextForHook is always populated
Merge the two if/else prompt-submission branches into a unified path that always calls installRuntimeContextMessageForPrompt
src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/sanitize-user-facing-text.ts — strip the placeholder when it appears standalone as a defense-in-depth safety net
src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.sanitizeuserfacingtext.test.ts — add test covering the new sanitize rule
User Impact
Before: "Continue the OpenClaw runtime event." appears in conversations as a user message
After: The placeholder is hidden from all user-visible surfaces (UI transcript, compaction summaries, trajectory exports)
Evidence

pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.sanitizeuserfacingtext.test.ts
  119 passed (new: "strips the runtime-only user prompt placeholder when it appears standalone")

pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/runtime-context-prompt.test.ts
  17 passed

pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts
  62 passed

pnpm test src/agents/internal-runtime-context.test.ts
  5 passed

target.title: fix(googlechat): replace unbounded response.json() with readProviderJsonResponse
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

`extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts` (`readGoogleChatJsonResponse`) and `extensions/googlechat/src/auth.ts` (`readGoogleChatCertsResponse`) both call `await response.json()` with no byte-level cap. A hostile or malfunctioning Google Chat API endpoint (or an intermediary) can return an oversized JSON body that exhausts process memory during JSON parsing.

This is the same bounded-read pattern Alix-007 applied to 15+ other extensions (`readProviderJsonResponse`, 16 MiB cap). Google Chat was one of the remaining unbounded `.json()` call sites.

## Changes

- `extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts:15-19` — `readGoogleChatJsonResponse` now delegates to `readProviderJsonResponse<T>(response, label)`. The local `try { return await response.json() } catch (cause) { throw new Error(..., { cause }) }` wrapper is replaced by the SDK helper which provides the same error shape (`${label}: malformed JSON response`) plus a 16 MiB byte cap.
- `extensions/googlechat/src/auth.ts:19-25` — `readGoogleChatCertsResponse` now delegates to `readProviderJsonResponse<Record<string, string>>(response, "Google Chat cert fetch failed")`. Error message preserved identically.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: unbounded `response.json()` on Google Chat API and cert HTTP responses; after the fix reads are capped at 16 MiB.
- **Real environment tested**: a real `node:http` server bound to `127.0.0.1` returning a JSON body exceeding 16 MiB (20 MiB), plus an 8 MiB negative-control body, plus a small valid Google Chat JSON response. Drives the production `readProviderJsonResponse` over a real TCP socket. Node v22.22.0.
- **Exact steps**: `node --import tsx _proof_googlechat_json.mts` (proof script kept local-only, not committed).
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```
  === Google Chat JSON response bounded read (cap=16777216) ===
  
  PASS  Google Chat JSON bounded: overflow; bytesSent=20971709; server.aborted=true
  PASS  Google Chat JSON negative control: small body parsed (status=ok); cap did not trigger
  PASS  Google Chat JSON happy path: valid response parsed (text="hello")
  
  === All Google Chat JSON bounded-read assertions passed ===
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: `readProviderJsonResponse` threw the canonical overflow error when JSON body exceeded 16 MiB. Server-side `socket.bytesWritten` confirmed ~20 MiB was sent before cancellation. Negative control and happy path parsed correctly.
- **What was not tested**: live Google Chat API call (the proof exercises the same production SDK helper); cross-platform Node differences.

## Out of scope

- `extensions/googlechat/src/api.ts:117` (`fetchBuffer`) already uses `readResponseWithLimit` for media downloads — not affected.
- Other extensions (image-generation, etc.) — separate PR (#3f).

## Risk

- **Very low**: swap from a local `(await response.json()) as T` to `readProviderJsonResponse<T>(response, label)`. Error messages are preserved (`${label}: malformed JSON response`). The helper is already used by 15+ extensions. The byte cap (16 MiB, default) is invisible to normal-sized responses.
- **No SDK promotion**: `readProviderJsonResponse` has been in `openclaw/plugin-sdk/provider-http` since the Alix-007 bounded-read sweep.

## Diff stats

```
2 files changed, 7 insertions(+), 10 deletions(-)
```

Net code change (excl. import lines): **~3 lines deleted** (the local try/catch wrappers removed).

target.title: fix(web-fetch): extract full HTML body content
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes #82685 by treating title-only Readability output as failed body extraction instead of returning only the page title.
- Normalizes title-only comparison through plain-text markdown stripping, so markdown-mode headings like `# Sample Page` also fall through instead of being returned as body content.
- Preserves the configured-provider-before-raw-HTML order for title-only Readability pages, so existing provider routing remains the first recovery path when configured.
- Hardens the runtime body scanner so fake `<body>` tags inside comments, raw-text containers, and inert/nested `<template>` regions do not replace the real visible document body.
- Keeps the change scoped to the `web_fetch` runtime extraction path; shared HTML-to-markdown helpers, provider configuration, network policy, and public API shape are unchanged.

What problem does this PR solve?

`web_fetch` could accept Readability output that only contained the page title as successful body extraction. In default markdown mode, that included heading-only output such as `# Sample Page`, because the title comparison did not strip markdown before deciding whether extraction only returned the title. In that state, users and agents could receive only the title instead of visible page content. The runtime body scanner also needed to preserve the document-body boundary while skipping HTML regions that can legally contain literal fake body markup but are not the visible body.

Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is user-visible: downstream tool output is misleading when `web_fetch` returns title-only text or hidden/inert fake body content instead of the actual page body.

What is the intended outcome?

This is a root-cause extraction-state fix: title-only Readability output no longer completes extraction, and body-boundary scanning now enforces the browser-visible body invariant before raw HTML text is produced. Successful behavior is provider output when a configured provider handles the title-only page, or visible body text from local raw HTML cleanup when no provider output is available.

What is intentionally out of scope?

This PR does not change shared `htmlToMarkdown`, provider configuration, network/auth/security policy, migrations, schema, public API shape, or the provider catalog. It only changes the `web_fetch` runtime extraction decision and focused regression coverage for that path.

What should reviewers focus on?

Please review the source-of-truth boundary between local Readability extraction, configured provider routing, and runtime-local raw HTML body cleanup. The provider compatibility boundary is preserved: title-only pages still try configured provider routing before local raw HTML cleanup, while scanner hardening only affects the local cleanup path when it is reached.

## Linked context

- Closes #82685.
- Updates PR #89028 after review feedback called out missing real-behavior proof, raw-text/template scanner gaps, and provider compatibility risk.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `web_fetch` no longer returns only the page title when Readability emits title-only text; it continues extraction and returns visible body content when no configured provider output is available.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw `web_fetch` tool execution with the real Readability extractor and a deterministic HTML fetch fixture. External network access was not required for this proof.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: ran a Node proof script that calls the real Readability extractor, then calls `createWebFetchTool().execute` with `{ extractMode: 'markdown' }` against the same title-only fixture with provider fetching disabled.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

  ```text
  web_fetch title-only fallback proof
  fixture url: https://fixture.openclaw.test/title-only-template-raw-text
  readability.title: "Sample Page"
  readability.text: "Sample Page"
  readability.titleOnly: true
  web_fetch.status: 200
  web_fetch.contentType: text/html
  web_fetch.extractor: raw-html
  web_fetch.title.hasSamplePage: true
  web_fetch.text.hasBodyMarker: true
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the fixture forced title-only Readability output, and with no configured provider output available, the `web_fetch` result used `raw-html` with the expected body marker present.
- What was not tested: the exact original reporter page, because the issue did not include a concrete URL or full HTML fixture.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the live proof uses a deterministic fixture so the title-only condition and expected body marker are reproducible. The configured-provider order is covered by a focused regression test that asserts provider output wins before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the new focused regressions failed before this fix: the nested-template raw-text case surfaced fake script body text, and the provider-order guard caught the intermediate regression where local raw HTML cleanup ran before the configured provider.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "falls back to body HTML when markdown readability returns only the page title heading"` — failed before the latest repair with `expected 'readability' to be 'raw-html'`, then passed after markdown-stripping title comparison.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts -t "tries provider fallback before body HTML when readability returns only the page title"` — passed; configured provider output still wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts` — passed, 32 tests.

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added markdown-mode title-only coverage where Readability returns `# Sample Page`, proving heading-only output is treated as incomplete extraction and falls through to visible body HTML when no provider output is available.
- Added coverage for fake body tags inside raw-text content nested within inert templates, proving the scanner returns the real body marker and excludes the fake body text.
- Restored title-only/provider coverage so configured provider output is used before local raw HTML cleanup when a provider is configured.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- The markdown-heading title-only regression initially returned the `readability` result `# Sample Page` instead of continuing to `raw-html` body content.
- The nested-template raw-text regression initially returned fake script body text instead of the real body marker.
- The provider-order guard caught an intermediate regression where recoverable local body HTML bypassed the configured provider.

If no test was added, why not?

- Not applicable; focused regression coverage was added/updated for the scanner gap and provider compatibility boundary.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. For title-only Readability pages, `web_fetch` now continues extraction instead of stopping at the title. Configured provider order is preserved, and local raw HTML body cleanup is used only when provider output is unavailable.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No expansion of capability. The change stays inside `web_fetch` extraction and does not broaden network access, auth, secrets, or tool execution.

What is the highest-risk area?

Compatibility for title-only pages: `web_fetch` now treats title-only Readability as incomplete and may proceed to configured provider routing or local raw HTML cleanup instead of returning only the title.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The canonical source of truth remains `web_fetch` runtime extraction: Readability first, configured provider routing second, local raw HTML cleanup last for HTML extraction failures.
- The change is limited to title-only Readability output after plain-text markdown normalization; normal successful Readability output is unchanged.
- The focused provider regression asserts that configured provider output still wins before local raw HTML cleanup.
- Raw HTML cleanup stays runtime-local to `web_fetch`; shared conversion helpers and public API shape are unchanged.
- Related open PR scan result: PR #89028 is the active repair path for #82685, and current main did not already contain this title-only/body-boundary fix when this update started.
- Patch-quality note: the added `null`/`undefined` parser results are explicit scanner miss signals for malformed/non-tag input, not symptom masking; they allow scanning to continue until the real body boundary is found or no body is present.
- Size note: the diff is confined to one runtime file and its focused tests; the line count is mostly regression coverage for existing review findings in this PR.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the updated branch is submitted and CI settles.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Maintainer: review the title-only extraction behavior with provider-first compatibility preserved.
- CI: rerun on the updated PR branch after submission.
- External proof: no exact reporter URL/HTML was available, so proof uses representative deterministic HTML that reproduces the title-only/body-cleanup invariant.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 fixed: the author-side code/test changes for ClawSweeper's waiting-on-author review have been applied; after submission, ClawSweeper can re-evaluate the status label.
- RF-002/RF-004/RF-008/RF-009 fixed: title-only comparison now strips markdown through plain-text normalization before comparing Readability output with the page title.
- RF-003/RF-007 stale/current-state addressed: the previously reported `checks-node-compact-small-whole-2` failure is no longer visible on the latest collected check rollup, and CI will rerun on the updated PR branch after submission.
- RF-005 addressed as maintainer decision context: provider-first order remains intentional and documented as the compatibility/cost tradeoff reviewers should evaluate.
- RF-006 addressed: the PR body states that the exact reporter URL/HTML was unavailable and the proof uses a deterministic representative fixture.
- RF-010 fixed: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/web-tools.fetch.test.ts` passed locally, 32 tests.
- RF-011 fixed: the provider-before-body fallback order regression passed after the markdown-title repair.
- RF-012 fixed: the new markdown-heading title-only regression failed before the repair with `expected 'readability' to be 'raw-html'` and passed after the repair.
